notes.anchor: on the eighth day before the Ides of February
notes.note: By inclusive Roman reckoning, 8 February 56 BC. On this day Pompey, speaking for Milo before the people, was shouted down and Cato attacked him in the Senate --- the riot that broke the momentum of the Egyptian command.

The religio raised by a verse of the Sibylline Books, which forbade restoring the Egyptian king with an army; it blocked entrusting the command to anyone for an armed restoration, and so kept Lentulus's claim formally intact.

notes.anchor: that the king, when he understands
notes.note: Ptolemy XII Auletes, the exiled king of Egypt then resident at Rome, whose restoration is the whole subject of the correspondence. Cicero's hope is that he will give up on Pompey and turn to Lentulus.
Tarditas et taciturnitas: Cicero's repeated complaint about Pompey's habit of keeping his intentions cryptic and acting only slowly --- a trait that here frustrates the Egyptian plan.
Levissimus homo: the tribune C. Porcius Cato, whose attacks Cicero dismisses as the bluster of a lightweight. Cicero predicts his impetus will soon be broken.
